Every backend developer has experienced the same frustrating situation at some point in their career. A feature works perfectly during development, where the database contains only a few thousand records. Queries return almost instantly, API responses are fast, and everything appears production-ready. However, a few months after deployment, users begin complaining that pages take several seconds to load. Product searches become sluggish, dashboards freeze while loading reports, and customer lookups suddenly feel painfully slow. Surprisingly, the application code hasn’t changed much — the only thing that has changed is the amount of data.
This is where one of the most important concepts in database engineering comes into the picture: Database Indexing.
Indexing is often introduced as a simple performance optimization, but in reality, it is one of the primary reasons modern databases can efficiently handle billions of records. Companies like Amazon, Google, Netflix, Uber, and Instagram rely heavily on indexes to retrieve information in milliseconds despite storing enormous amounts of data. Without indexes, many of the applications we use every day would simply become unusable as their databases continued to grow.
In this article, we’ll explore what database indexes actually are, why they exist, how they work internally, why databases rely on B+ Trees instead of simpler data structures, how query optimizers use indexes, and the situations where indexes can surprisingly reduce performance instead of improving it.
Why Databases Become Slow
To appreciate why indexes are necessary, we first need to understand how a database searches for information when no index exists.
Imagine a Users table containing one hundred million records. A customer logs into an application, and the backend executes a query to locate the user’s information using their email address. If no index exists on the email column, the database has no intelligent way of locating that specific row. It must begin at the first row, compare the email address, move to the second row, compare again, and continue this process until the matching record is found.
This operation is known as a Full Table Scan, sometimes referred to as a Sequential Scan.
For a table containing only a few thousand records, this approach works reasonably well because scanning the entire table takes very little time. However, databases powering modern applications rarely contain thousands of rows. They often contain hundreds of millions or even billions. As datasets continue growing, the amount of unnecessary work performed during every search increases proportionally.
This explains why applications often perform well during development but gradually slow down in production. The algorithm used by the database has not changed; only the amount of data it must inspect has increased.
Understanding the Problem Through a Simple Analogy
Imagine walking into a library containing ten million books.
Suppose someone asks you to find a book titled Distributed Systems Engineering.
Without any catalog, your only option is to begin at the first shelf, examine every book individually, and continue until you eventually locate the correct one. The larger the library becomes, the longer this process takes.
Now imagine the same library provides a catalog organized alphabetically. Instead of searching every shelf, you first locate the book’s title inside the catalog, discover its shelf number, and immediately walk to the correct location.
The second approach is dramatically faster because you no longer search every book you search a much smaller, organized data structure.
That catalog is exactly what a database index represents. Instead of searching the entire table, the database searches a much smaller structure containing only indexed values and pointers to the actual rows.
What Exactly Is a Database Index?
A database index is an additional data structure maintained separately from the actual table. Rather than storing every column of every record, it stores only the indexed values along with references pointing to their corresponding rows.
Whenever a query searches for an indexed column, the database first navigates through the index to locate the desired record and then retrieves the complete row from the table.
This dramatically reduces the number of disk pages that must be accessed.
Think of an index as a shortcut.
Without the shortcut, the database walks through the entire city.
With the shortcut, it already knows exactly where the destination is located.
Of course, shortcuts are not free. Maintaining this additional structure requires extra storage and additional work whenever data changes. This trade-off between faster reads and slower writes lies at the heart of database indexing.
Why Not Simply Use Binary Search?
At this point, many developers naturally ask an interesting question.
“If the data is sorted, why can’t the database simply use Binary Search?”
Binary Search certainly performs much better than scanning every record. However, Binary Search requires the underlying data to remain perfectly ordered. Real-world databases are constantly receiving new inserts, updates, and deletions. Maintaining perfect ordering after every modification would require continuously rearranging enormous portions of the table.
Even Binary Search Trees are not suitable for databases because they can become highly unbalanced. If values are inserted in sorted order, a Binary Search Tree gradually degenerates into a linked list, causing search performance to fall back to linear time.
Databases needed a data structure specifically designed for massive datasets stored on disk rather than in memory.
That solution became the B+ Tree.
Why Every Relational Database Loves B+ Trees
Almost every relational database including PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle, and MariaDB uses B+ Trees as its default indexing structure.
Unlike Binary Search Trees, B+ Trees remain balanced regardless of insertion order. Every search travels approximately the same number of levels before reaching the desired record, guaranteeing predictable performance even as the database grows.
Another major advantage is that each node stores many keys instead of just one. Since database pages are typically several kilobytes in size, storing hundreds of keys inside a single node dramatically reduces the overall height of the tree. Fewer levels mean fewer disk accesses, and since disk operations are among the slowest operations a database performs, minimizing them significantly improves query performance.
B+ Trees also store all actual records in their leaf nodes while internal nodes contain only navigation keys. These leaf nodes are linked together sequentially, making range queries extremely efficient. When searching for all products priced between ₹500 and ₹1000, the database locates the first matching value and simply walks through neighboring leaf nodes instead of repeatedly traversing the tree.
This combination of balanced structure, minimal disk access, and efficient range traversal explains why B+ Trees have become the industry standard for database indexing.
Different Types of Indexes
Although B+ Tree indexes are the default choice, modern databases provide several indexing strategies because different workloads require different optimizations.
A clustered index determines the physical order in which rows are stored inside the table. Since the actual data follows the order of the index, only one clustered index can exist for a table. This arrangement makes range queries exceptionally efficient because related records remain physically adjacent on disk.
Non-clustered indexes, on the other hand, store indexed values separately from the actual table. Instead of containing complete rows, they maintain pointers to the corresponding records. This allows multiple non-clustered indexes to exist simultaneously, each optimized for different query patterns.
Composite indexes combine multiple columns into a single index. Imagine an e-commerce platform where customers frequently filter products by category, brand, and price. Rather than maintaining separate indexes for each column, a composite index stores these values together. However, understanding the leftmost prefix rule becomes essential because the database can only efficiently utilize the index when queries begin with the leading indexed columns.
Hash indexes represent another specialized approach. Instead of maintaining sorted keys, they compute a hash value for each indexed column. This makes equality lookups extremely fast but sacrifices the ability to perform range queries or sorting operations.
Some databases also provide Bitmap Indexes for analytical workloads and Full-Text Indexes for searching documents, articles, or product descriptions. Each indexing strategy exists because different applications solve different problems.
How the Query Optimizer Decides
One common misconception is that creating an index guarantees the database will always use it. In reality, every SQL query first passes through the database’s Query Optimizer.
The optimizer estimates multiple execution plans before choosing the cheapest one. It considers factors such as table size, data distribution, index selectivity, available statistics, and estimated I/O costs. Suppose a query requests 95% of all rows in a table.
Although an index exists, using it would require jumping back and forth between the index and the table for nearly every record. In this situation, reading the entire table sequentially is actually faster. For this reason, databases sometimes ignore indexes completely and perform a Full Table Scan instead. This surprises many developers, but it highlights an important lesson: indexes are optimization tools, not mandatory execution paths.
When Indexes Become a Problem
Many beginners assume that adding indexes to every column will automatically improve performance.
The opposite is often true.
Every INSERT operation must not only write the new row into the table but also update every associated index. UPDATE operations become more expensive because indexed values may need to be repositioned inside the tree. DELETE operations similarly require removing entries from every index.
As the number of indexes grows, write performance gradually decreases.
Indexes also consume additional storage space. Large production databases often allocate hundreds of gigabytes exclusively for indexes.
Good indexing therefore requires balance. The objective is not to create the maximum number of indexes but to create the minimum number that effectively supports the application’s most important queries.
Database Indexing in Real-World Systems
Every major technology company depends heavily on indexing. Amazon indexes product names, brands, categories, prices, and seller information to provide nearly instant product searches. Banking systems index account numbers and transaction identifiers because financial lookups must complete within milliseconds. Instagram indexes usernames, hashtags, and timestamps to generate user feeds efficiently. Ride-sharing platforms index geographical coordinates to identify nearby drivers in real time, while analytics systems use specialized indexes to accelerate reporting across billions of historical records.
Although users rarely notice indexes directly, nearly every fast application depends on them.
Conclusion
Database indexing is much more than a performance optimization it is one of the fundamental engineering techniques that allows modern software systems to scale. Without indexes, databases would spend most of their time scanning enormous tables, making applications progressively slower as data volumes increased. By introducing carefully designed data structures such as B+ Trees, databases dramatically reduce search complexity, minimize disk access, and retrieve information with remarkabl
